message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Introduction</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2), which is responsible for the COVID-19 pandemic, remains viable and therefore potentially infectious on several materials. One strategy to discourage the fomite-mediated spread of COVID-19 is the development of materials whose surface chemistry can spontaneously inactivate SARS-CoV-2. Silicon nitride (Si
                    <jats:sub>3</jats:sub>
                    N
                    <jats:sub>4</jats:sub>
                    ), a material used in spine fusion surgery, is one such candidate because it has been shown to inactivate several bacterial species and viral strains. This study hypothesized that contact with Si
                    <jats:sub>3</jats:sub>
                    N
                    <jats:sub>4</jats:sub>
                    would inactivate SARS-CoV-2, while mammalian cells would remain unaffected.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Materials</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    SARS-CoV-2 virions (2×10
                    <jats:sup>4</jats:sup>
                    PFU/mL diluted in growth media) were exposed to 5, 10, 15, and 20% (w/v) of an aqueous suspension of sintered Si
                    <jats:sub>3</jats:sub>
                    N
                    <jats:sub>4</jats:sub>
                    particles for durations of 1, 5, and 10 minutes, respectively. Before exposure to the virus, cytotoxicity testing of Si
                    <jats:sub>3</jats:sub>
                    N
                    <jats:sub>4</jats:sub>
                    alone was assessed in Vero cells at 24 and 48 hour post-exposure times. Following each exposure to Si
                    <jats:sub>3</jats:sub>
                    N
                    <jats:sub>4</jats:sub>
                    , the remaining infectious virus was quantitated by plaque assay.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Vero cell viability increased at 5% and 10% (w/v) concentrations of Si
                    <jats:sub>3</jats:sub>
                    N
                    <jats:sub>4</jats:sub>
                    at exposure times up to 10 minutes, and there was only minimal impact on cell health and viability up to 20% (w/v). However, the SARS-CoV-2 titers were markedly reduced when exposed to all concentrations of Si
                    <jats:sub>3</jats:sub>
                    N
                    <jats:sub>4</jats:sub>
                    ; the reduction in viral titers was between 85% - 99.6%, depending on the dose and duration of exposure.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Si
                    <jats:sub>3</jats:sub>
                    N
                    <jats:sub>4</jats:sub>
                    was non-toxic to the Vero cells while showing strong antiviral activity against SARS-CoV-2. The viricidal effect increased with increasing concentrations of Si
                    <jats:sub>3</jats:sub>
                    N
                    <jats:sub>4</jats:sub>
                    and longer duration of exposure. Surface treatment strategies based on Si
                    <jats:sub>3</jats:sub>
                    N
                    <jats:sub>4</jats:sub>
                    may offer novel methods to discourage SARS-CoV-2 persistence and infectivity on surfaces and discourage the spread of COVID-19.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Silicon Nitride Inactivates SARS-CoV-2 in vitro

reference.article-title: Stability of SARS-CoV-2 in Different Environmental Conditions
Efficacy of Various Disinfectants Against SARS Coronavirus
